discussion_comments.comment_body:
Well, I don't think they're mathematically equivalent, at least.

In the original method we subtract the orthogonal portion of the refusal direction, so activations become weaker for weight between [0.8, 1.0] and stronger again for weight between [1.0, 1.5].

With magnitude preservation, we preserve the original activation strengths regardless of the weight, which means the modifications can only affect the alignment with the (negated) refusal direction. Because the orthogonal component of the refusal direction is subtracted along the columns, but the renormalization is done along the rows, it isn't exactly equivalent to a degree of rotation, but it does still reduce the change to a directional one.

Honestly though, I find it pretty hard to reason about. As a modification of the technique, it could also be interesting to preserve the magnitudes along the columns, turning the updates into genuine rotations. Then re-normalize along the rows and restore the activation strengths after that (which would once again change the alignment of the columns, but in a different way).

I was stuck on it for quite a while and tried to get several AIs to tell me what the heck they might have meant with biprojection. But I think what I said is it. [Their code](https://github.com/jim-plus/llm-abliteration/blob/main/sharded_ablate.py#L318) (which I only looked at just now) appears to confirm this:

```python
                    refusal_dir = measures[f'refuse_{measurement}'].float()
                    harmless_dir = measures[f'harmless_{layer}'].float()

                    if projected:
                        # Here we orthogonalize refusal with respect to harmless direction.
                        # We compute the second orthogonalized vector from Gram-Schmitt orthonormalization.

                        # Normalize harmless direction
                        harmless_normalized = torch.nn.functional.normalize(harmless_dir, dim=0)

                        # Project and subtract to refine refusal direction
                        projection_scalar = refusal_dir @ harmless_normalized
                        refined_refusal_dir = refusal_dir - projection_scalar * harmless_normalized
                        refusal_dir = refined_refusal_dir.to(precision)
```

So they use some pre-determined refusal direction, but refine it by subtracting the component aligned with the harmless direction of the layer itself (which I think ultimately amounts to the same thing as what my code does).

> Right, but why? Why is the refusal direction global, but the harmlessness direction is per-layer? What is the logic behind this?

I can only speculate, but I think the idea is that we want to remove the harmful *component* of a singular refusal direction across multiple layers, while leaving each layer's *harmless* direction alone as much as possible. In the original article, the harmful component was extracted relative to the source layer, but maybe the idea is that each layer's notion of harmlessness is different.

But maybe it's BS, and the reason they had to invent magnitude preservation is because the theory doesn't hold up (they did say that results were worse *without* magnitude preservation).

> Also, where is the "bi" part in "biprojection"? AFAICT, we are only projecting _once_, so why "biprojection"?

The only thing I can think of is that it's "bi" because it involves 2 layers (the source and the target). A misnomer in that case, IMO. Technically I suppose we also project onto the columns of the weight matrix itself in order to remove the harmful component, but that's true regardless so it wouldn't make sense for that to be the reason.

+                        # Subtract the original matrix to turn W into a delta.
+                        W = W - W_org
+                        # Use a low-rank SVD to get an approximation of the matrix.
+                        r = self.lora_rank
+                        U, S, Vh = torch.svd_lowrank(W, q=2 * r + 4, niter=6)
+                        # Truncate it to the part we want to store in the LoRA adapter.
+                        # Note: svd_lowrank actually returns V, so transpose it to get Vh.
+                        U = U[:, :r]
+                        S = S[:r]
+                        Vh = Vh[:, :r].T
+                        # Transfer it into the LoRA adapter components.
+                        sqrt_S = torch.sqrt(S)
+                        lora_B = U @ torch.diag(sqrt_S)
+                        lora_A = torch.diag(sqrt_S) @ Vh
discussion_comments.comment_body: `U` and `V` are balanced in the sense that all the vectors in `U` and `V` have norm 1 (forming an orthonormal basis). Spreading `S` over both components keeps them balanced. Theoretically an extreme imbalance could cause issues with numerical stability, or risk losing precision in the smaller values of `S` (if they're very close to 0).

I've implemented a hybrid approach to handle this:

The solution: The code now checks if a module has LoRA adapters (hasattr(module, "lora_A")) before attempting LoRA abliteration. If the module doesn't have LoRA adapters (like nn.Parameter in GPT-OSS), it falls back to direct weight modification using the projector matrix approach from the original implementation.

Key changes:

if self.settings.use_lora and hasattr(module, "lora_A"):
    # LoRA abliteration for modules with adapters
    ...
else:
    # Direct weight modification for nn.Parameter or non-LoRA mode
    matrix = module.weight if hasattr(module, "weight") else module
    ...

This means:

Models with proper modules (most models): Use LoRA adapters when use_lora=True
GPT-OSS and similar models: Automatically fall back to direct weight modification, even in LoRA mode
Non-LoRA mode: Works as before with direct modification
The hybrid approach ensures compatibility across all model architectures while still leveraging LoRA's memory efficiency where possible.

Note: These changes are currently untested on GPT-OSS specifically, as I don't have access to test it. I'd appreciate feedback if you have the opportunity to verify the behavior.

Does this address your concern?

edit: Quick note on GPT-OSS usage:
I noticed that converted versions like `unsloth/gpt-oss-20b-BF16` are available, which (should, I guess) map the architecture to standard modules.
This means that users loading these versions will actually benefit from the **LoRA path** (efficient 4-bit loading + adapters), as PEFT handles them correctly.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Yes, exactly. When you load a model with load_in_4bit=True and set bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16, BitsAndBytes:

- Stores weights in packed 4-bit format (NF4).
- On each forward pass, dequantizes the weights to BF16 (the compute_dtype) for the actual matrix multiplication.
- Quantizes them back to 4-bit after the operation (if needed).

The dtype="auto" passed to AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained determines the dtype for non-quantized parts (like embeddings, layer norms). BitsAndBytes handles the quantized layers separately using compute_dtype.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
merge_and_unload() modifies self.model in-place, destroying the LoRA adapter layers. This flag signals 
reset_model_for_trial() to perform a full model reload (instead of just resetting adapters) if the user runs another trial after saving/uploading. Without this, subsequent trials would crash with AttributeError: 'Linear' object has no attribute 'lora_A'

> merge_and_unload() modifies self.model in-place

Does it? [The documentation](https://huggingface.co/docs/peft/main/en/package_reference/tuners#peft.tuners.tuners_utils.BaseTuner.merge_and_unload) explicitly states (emphasis added):

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Current status: Crashes on GPT-OSS because PEFT cannot wrap raw nn.Parameter tensors (used in MLPs).

Two viable paths forward:

1. Skip non-adapter layers:
Check hasattr(module, "lora_A"). GPT-OSS mlp.down_proj will be ignored (attention-only ablation).

2. Hybrid / Fallback:
Re-implement direct weight modification for raw tensors.


Which one do you want?

---

Addendum:
Ironically, layer scanning suggests that mlp.down_proj contributes negligible refusal signal in GPT-OSS (divergence is ~2100x lower than in attn.o_proj).
Consequently, Option 1 (Skipping non-LoRA layers) would practically yield an optimal result for this specific architecture, as MLP intervention appears to be structural noise here.
Reference data: [https://huggingface.co/arnomatic/gpt-oss-20b-heretic-scanner-V1-2](https://www.google.com/url?sa=E&q=https%3A%2F%2Fhuggingface.co%2Farnomatic%2Fgpt-oss-20b-heretic-scanner-V1-2)

**Sure, that works for gpt-oss, but it's not a permanent solution.**

---
Context on Architecture:
It is also worth noting that GPT-OSS is structurally an outlier (using raw 3D tensors for experts). The vast majority of modern standard architectures (~95% like Llama, Mistral, Gemma) use standard nn.Linear modules that PEFT handles natively without issues.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
you are correct that `auto `handles native `bfloat16 `models. The reason for adding `bfloat16 `to the list is to serve as a fallback for native `float32 `models.

If auto is used for such models (float32) can cause out of memory errors in low end env, the next fallback is `float16`. If float16 suffers from numerical instability (common in some models), the next fallback would be float32 again (which OOMs)

that single line doesn't break anything at all, it's only useful.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
instability won't trigger the fallback. right

However, `float16 `can raise explicit exceptions during loading on certain hardware/backends, such as 
```bash
RuntimeError: "LayerNormKernelImpl" not implemented for 'Half'
OR
RuntimeError: probability tensor contains either inf, nan or element < 0
(which is what the comment references)
```
. In those specific cases where float16 crashes with an error, having `bfloat16 `as the next fallback allows us to try a low-precision format before even trying to the memory-heavy `float32`

This was also suggested by a user on that Seperate Issue
https://github.com/meta-llama/llama/issues/380#issuecomment-1656569519
